title: Why the West stopped making land - Works in Progress Magazine
gist: The West abruptly stopped reclaiming land in the 1970s not because the easy sites ran out or transportation made it unnecessary, but because a wave of 1970s environmental laws (NEPA, Clean Water Act, ESA) made reshaping coastlines prohibitively slow and expensive. With urban land values now 70x reclamation cost in places, reforming those laws could unlock hundreds of thousands of homes, new airports and coastal flood protection—while China, Singapore and Japan keep reclaiming at scale.

insights.claim: Roughly 8% of the land in America's major coastal cities was underwater in the 1890s and has since been reclaimed, including the sites of Newark, Logan and SFO airports and neighborhoods like San Francisco's Financial District and Boston's Back Bay.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Establishes that prime urban real estate is routinely manufactured—framing reclamation as a latent supply lever for land-constrained metros.
insights.quote: around eight percent of the land in America’s major coastal cities was underwater in the 1890s and has since been reclaimed
insights.locator: Some of America’s most famous land

insights.claim: Since Battery Park City was completed in 1976 there has not been a single major urban land reclamation project in the United States, despite land values being up to 30x their 1950 levels and high-tide flooding four to eight times more frequent.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: A 50-year cessation amid soaring land scarcity signals a policy-created supply gap—reform here is a potential structural unlock for urban housing.
insights.quote: Since the completion of Battery Park City in 1976, there has not been a single major urban land reclamation project in the United States
insights.locator: Today, reclamation should be more common than ever

insights.claim: Over a quarter of Manhattan's land was reclaimed before 1979, and cities like Boston and Charleston roughly doubled in size through reclamation.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Quantifies how much of today's most valuable urban land is reclaimed—proof of concept that the practice scales to major cities.
insights.quote: over a quarter of Manhattan’s land was reclaimed before 1979
insights.locator: Has transportation made land reclamation unnecessary? / Manhattan

themes.theme: Debunking the 'ran out of good spots' explanation

insights.claim: China still leads the world in reclamation despite a 2,500-year history, with nearly 80% of all land reclaimed in the 21st century and Chinese cities over one million people reclaiming an area equal to 39 Manhattans since 2000.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Refutes resource exhaustion as the cause—if millennia-old China hasn't run out, the US scarcity story is about law, not geology.
insights.quote: China still leads the world in land reclamation, with close to 80 percent of all land reclaimed in the twenty-first century
insights.locator: Have we run out of good spots? / China

insights.claim: Hundreds of square miles of shallow water near US cities remain easily reclaimable: two-thirds of San Francisco Bay is shallower than Back Bay's 20-foot depth, the South Bay is under six feet, and most of New York harbor outside channels is under ten feet.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Maps concrete, technically-easy reclamation candidates adjacent to the most valuable US real estate—the supply is physically there.
insights.quote: Two thirds of the San Francisco Bay is shallower than this. The South Bay, adjoining Silicon Valley, is less than six feet deep.
insights.locator: Have we run out of good spots? / depths

insights.claim: Depth and soil are not hard limits when land values justify the cost: Monaco's Mareterra dredged to 164 feet, and Singapore's Jurong Island was built over 50 feet of water atop 50 feet of soft marine clay.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Shows technical difficulty is an economic, not absolute, barrier—high land values make even hard sites pencil out, widening the candidate set.
insights.quote: Monaco’s recent Mareterra project expanded the country’s land area by three percent by dredging down to 164 feet.
insights.locator: Have we run out of good spots? / Monaco Singapore

insights.claim: Transportation once substituted for reclamation cheaply—New York's 1913 subway extension unlocked land at ~$65,000/acre versus $400,000/acre for Boston's 1860s Back Bay fill—but that substitute has stopped improving.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Explains the historical decline mechanically, then sets up why it no longer holds—transport-based land creation has hit diminishing returns.
insights.quote: this form of land reclamation cost only $65,000 per acre in 2025 dollars, five to ten times less than land reclamation from the sea
insights.locator: Has transportation made land reclamation unnecessary? / subway

insights.claim: Transportation has stagnated: average commute times rose a quarter since 1980, highway construction now costs at least four times per mile what it did in 1960, and the New York metro edge is already 40 miles (over an hour) from Midtown.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Documents that the cheap-substitute era is over, restoring reclamation's relative attractiveness—macro tailwind for land-creation reform.
insights.quote: highway construction costs at least four times as much per mile as it did in 1960
insights.locator: Has transportation made land reclamation unnecessary? / stagnation

insights.claim: Reclamation is now far below today's urban land values: a 1955 estimate to reclaim 23,000 acres on the west San Francisco Bay was ~$330,000/acre in 2025 dollars, versus an average $24 million/acre for single-family-zoned SF land today—more than 70x.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: A 70x value-to-cost gap is the economic core of the thesis—reclamation is wildly profitable on paper, so only law explains its absence.
insights.quote: the cost of an acre of single-family-zoned land in San Francisco County averages $24 million, more than 70 times the estimated reclamation cost
insights.locator: Has transportation made land reclamation unnecessary? / SF values

insights.claim: Since 1990 the post-WWII flight from city centers reversed and downtown land values have grown quickly despite continued suburban expansion, because cities concentrate jobs and generate knowledge spillovers.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Rising central-city premiums mean reclaimed downtown land is uniquely valuable—reinforcing the case for near-center land creation.
insights.quote: since 1990, this trend has reversed, and the value of land in city centers has grown quickly despite continuing suburban expansion
insights.locator: Has transportation made land reclamation unnecessary? / city value

themes.theme: 1970s environmental law as the real cause

insights.claim: Reclamation stopped virtually simultaneously across the US in the 1970s—of 37 cities with data, every one slowed after 1970—a sharp, synchronized halt inconsistent with gradual technological or geographic causes.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: The abrupt synchronized timing is the article's smoking gun pointing to a single common cause—federal law, not local conditions.
insights.quote: In the 1970s, reclamation stopped virtually simultaneously everywhere in the country.
insights.locator: How reclamation went extinct

insights.claim: The annual rate of US wetland loss in the 1960s was eight times higher than in the 1990s and twenty times higher than in the 2010s, marking a sudden 1970s break.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Independent wetland-loss data corroborates the abrupt 1970s policy break—strengthening the law-as-cause argument.
insights.quote: The annual rate of wetland loss in the 1960s was eight times higher than in the 1990s, and twenty times higher than in the 2010s.
insights.locator: How reclamation went extinct / wetland loss

insights.claim: A 1970s wave of laws—NEPA (1969), Clean Water Act (1972), Marine Mammal Protection Act (1972), Endangered Species Act (1973), RCRA (1976)—raised the cost of reshaping the landscape; the Clean Water Act forces every coastal reclamation through a federal permit and thus NEPA review.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Identifies the precise legal chokepoint—CWA permitting triggering NEPA—so reformers (and investors in land/infrastructure) know exactly what to change.
insights.quote: Land reclamation must always go through the NEPA procedure because the Clean Water Act requires a federal permit for any discharge of dredged material
insights.locator: How reclamation went extinct / the laws

insights.claim: Litigation, not the statute itself, made NEPA onerous: reports grew from ~10 pages to thousand-page behemoths, and Army Corps environmental impact statements for reclamation now take an average of six years with mitigation costs in the hundreds of millions.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Pinpoints litigation-driven process bloat as the lever—suggesting procedural reform (thresholds, litigation limits) could restore feasibility.
insights.quote: Environmental impact statements filed by the US Army Corps of Engineers, the agency that oversees the permits for land reclamation, take an average of six years to complete
insights.locator: How reclamation went extinct / NEPA litigation

insights.claim: The Craney Island extension in Norfolk illustrates the paralysis: a project easy by 1950s standards (original built in two years) has seen 28 years pass since its 1997 feasibility study with almost nothing built and a projected 2065 completion.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: A concrete ~70-year timeline shows how environmental process converts a 2-year job into a generational one—the cost of the status quo.
insights.quote: Twenty-eight years after the feasibility study began, almost nothing has been built. The project is expected to finish ‘on schedule’ in 2065.
insights.locator: How reclamation went extinct / Craney Island

themes.theme: Cross-country evidence and the Japan model

insights.claim: The same abrupt halt followed environmental laws across the West: Germany ended Wadden Sea reclamation in the late 1980s, France banned construction within 100m of the Mediterranean shore in the 1980s, and the Netherlands abandoned its planned Markerwaard polder, listing it as a protected area in 1992.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Multi-country replication of the law-then-halt pattern makes the causal claim robust—this is a Western regulatory phenomenon, not a US quirk.
insights.quote: What western nations shared was the rise of powerful environmental movements hostile to reshaping the landscape.
insights.locator: Evidence for environmental law’s role / other countries

insights.claim: Even the rare modern Western success, Rotterdam's Maasvlakte 2 port expansion, took 18 years including an 11-year planning/compliance period and a ~6,000-page environmental review.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Even successes are crippled by process timelines—setting realistic expectations for any reclamation revival in regulated jurisdictions.
insights.quote: took eighteen years to complete, including an eleven-year planning and environmental compliance period before construction began
insights.locator: Even the rare successes / Maasvlakte 2

insights.claim: Reclamation continues only where regulation is lighter—China reclaimed over 5,000 sq km in two decades (including a 500,000-person city outside Shanghai) and Singapore expanded its territory over a quarter since 1975.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Quantifies the geopolitical gap in land creation—lighter-regulation states are manufacturing buildable land the West cannot.
insights.quote: China has reclaimed over 5,000 square kilometers in the first two decades of this century
insights.locator: Reclamation has continued only in countries

insights.claim: Japan shows high environmental standards need not block reclamation: its NEPA-style law uses clear numeric thresholds for when reports are required and has courts that make agency decisions harder to overturn, so projects avoid the decades-long legal defense Western ones need.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Offers a concrete reform template—bright-line thresholds plus litigation deference—that preserves protection while restoring buildability.
insights.quote: Japan’s system has two advantages: clear numeric thresholds for when reports are required and courts that make it harder to overturn agency decisions
insights.locator: Japan does have high environmental standards

insights.claim: Real housing prices are at least five times higher today than in 1970 in New York, San Francisco, Boston and LA and three times higher nationally, so the value of construction foregone to environmental law has never been higher.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Quantifies the rising opportunity cost of inaction—reinforcing that the political and economic case for reform strengthens every year.
insights.quote: Real housing prices are at least five times higher today than in 1970 in New York, San Francisco, Boston, and LA
insights.locator: The wrong solution for an old problem / housing prices

insights.claim: Reclamation could simultaneously address 21st-century environmental goals because dense-city dwellers produce far less carbon per person than suburbanites, and in some cases the climate benefit of densification may by itself outweigh the loss of aquatic habitat.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Reframes reclamation as pro-environment, neutralizing the chief objection and broadening the coalition for reform.
insights.quote: city dwellers produce far less carbon per person than suburbanites, and reclaimed land can make coastal cities more resilient to natural disasters
insights.locator: The wrong solution for an old problem / environmental benefits

insights.claim: The author concludes that lifting legal barriers could build hundreds of thousands of homes near the centers of the most valuable cities, enable new airports, and protect low-lying coasts from sea-level rise—'the disappearance of land reclamation is a choice that we have the power to undo.'
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: States the headline policy upside; a US legal reform here would be a regime-change catalyst for coastal urban land supply and infrastructure.
insights.quote: The disappearance of land reclamation is a choice that we have the power to undo.
insights.locator: If the legal barriers to reclamation were lifted
